title: Political Staffer / Adviser
setting: Parliament House or Ministerial Office, Canberra + Electorate Office
companySize: A minister's office typically has 10-15 staff; a backbencher's office has 4-5
description: Political staffers are the engine room of Australian politics. They write the speeches, draft the policy, manage the media, handle the diary, and keep the show running. Ministerial advisers wield significant policy influence despite being unelected. Electorate officers are the frontline of constituent service. The hours are long, the pressure is intense, and the work is rarely visible — but nothing in politics happens without staffers making it happen.

schedule.activity: Wake up and scan the morning media — compile a media summary for your MP/minister before they wake up
schedule.note: Being across the media before your boss is the baseline. Surprising them with bad news they should have heard from you first is a career-limiting move.

schedule.activity: Send the morning brief — media summary, today's diary, key messages for scheduled interviews, flagged issues
Staff meeting — coordinate the day's priorities, assign tasks, discuss emerging issues
schedule.note: A well-run staff meeting takes 15 minutes. A badly run one takes an hour and achieves nothing.

schedule.activity: Draft a speech or talking points for the minister's 11am media conference
Briefing meeting with the department — receive policy advice, discuss implementation issues, negotiate positions
Accompany minister to media conference — hold the folder, whisper reminders, manage journalist access
schedule.note: You are invisible to the camera but essential to the performance.

schedule.activity: Handle a crisis — a constituent complaint has gone viral on social media. Draft a response, brief the minister, coordinate with the department
Lunch at desk — eat while responding to 50+ emails and managing the afternoon diary
schedule.note: Staffers do not have lunch breaks during sitting weeks.

schedule.activity: Prepare Question Time brief — anticipate questions, draft answers, prepare attack lines for the minister to use
Monitor Question Time from the advisers' box — pass notes, fact-check claims, prepare follow-up responses
schedule.note: The advisers' box behind the speaker is where staffers feed real-time information to their ministers.

schedule.activity: Debrief on the day — what went well, what went badly, what needs follow-up. Begin tomorrow's preparation
Attend a reception or event with the minister, brief them on who is in the room and what each person wants
schedule.note: A good staffer knows every face and every agenda in the room.

schedule.activity: Final email check, respond to overnight media queries, set morning alarm for 5:30am
schedule.note: Sitting weeks average 14-16 hour days. Non-sitting weeks are lighter but still demanding.

responsibilities: Manage the minister's or MP's daily operations — diary, correspondence, briefings
Draft speeches, media statements, talking points, and social media content
Liaise with government departments on policy implementation
Handle media enquiries and manage journalist relationships
Manage constituent casework and electorate office operations
Provide political advice and strategic counsel to your principal
Coordinate with party headquarters and other offices
challenges: Extremely long hours during sitting weeks — burnout is common
High-pressure environment with zero tolerance for mistakes
Working for a principal whose career depends on your performance
Limited job security — staffers can be let go when governments change
Dealing with demanding constituents, hostile media, and internal party politics simultaneously
rewards: Front-row seat to how government actually works
Direct influence on policy outcomes, even without elected office
Rapid professional development — you learn more in one year than most careers offer in five
Building a network that spans politics, media, public service, and industry
The foundation for your own political career or a transition to lobbying, media, or public service
careerPath: Intern or volunteer in an electorate office
Senior adviser or chief of staff
Transition to: elected office, public service, lobbying, journalism, or corporate affairs
